1: bārûk yhwh ṣûrî hamĕlammēd yādāy laqrāb ʾeṣbĕʿôtāy lammilḥāmāh — "blessed is the LORD, my rock, who trains my hands for war and my fingers for battle" — ṣûrî (my rock) — the divine epithet ṣûr (rock, cliff-face) emphasizes permanence and refuge (Ps 18:2; 19:14; 28:1). The psalm borrows heavily from Ps 18 — vv1-2 closely parallel Ps 18:2,34,47. hamĕlammēd yādāy laqrāb (who trains my hands for war) — the divine instructor of military skill: God himself is the one who makes David effective in battle. ʾeṣbĕʿôtāy lammilḥāmāh (my fingers for battle) — the detail of fingers alongside hands suggests precision skill (archery, swordsmanship) rather than mere brute strength.
2: ḥasdî ûmĕṣûdātî miśgabbî ûmĕpallĕṭî lî māginnî ûbô ḥāsîtî hārôdēd ʿammî taḥtāy — "he is my steadfast love and my fortress, my high tower and my deliverer, my shield and the one in whom I trust, who subdues peoples under me" — six divine titles stacked in rapid sequence: ḥasdî (my steadfast love / the source of my ḥesed), mĕṣûdātî (my stronghold/fortress), miśgabbî (my high place/refuge, from śāgab, to be high), mĕpallĕṭî (my deliverer), māginnî (my shield), and the one in whom I take refuge. The accumulation emphasizes that YHWH covers every dimension of protection — offensive, defensive, personal, and political.
3: yhwh mah-ʾādām wattēdāʿēhû ben-ʾĕnôš wattaḥaššĕbēhû — "LORD, what is a human being that you take notice of him, or the son of man that you think of him?" — near-verbatim parallel to Ps 8:4 (where the same question appears: mah-ʾĕnôš kî-tizkĕrennû ûben-ʾādām kî tipqĕdennû). But the registers differ: Ps 8 marvels at human dignity (the human is crowned with glory despite being small); Ps 144 marvels at divine attention to such an insubstantial creature (v4 explains why the question is shocking — humans are mere breath and shadow). Heb 2:6-8 cites Ps 8:4 and applies it to Christ; Ps 144's darker register of human transience is the foil that makes divine attention all the more remarkable.
4: ʾādām lahebel dāmāh yāmāyw kĕṣel ʿôbēr — "a human being is like a breath/vapor; his days pass like a shadow" — hebel (breath, vapor, vanity, futility) — the signature word of Ecclesiastes (Eccl 1:2: hebel hăbālîm, "vanity of vanities"); here applied to human existence itself. kĕṣel ʿôbēr (like a shadow passing) — the ṣēl (shadow) is the same word as YHWH's shade in Ps 121:5; here the insubstantial human shadow passes quickly. The theological point: because humans are so insubstantial, YHWH's attention to them is extraordinary condescension — yet he notices and cares (v3).
5: yhwh haṭ-šāmêkā wĕrēd gaʿ behārîm wĕyeʿăšānû — "bow your heavens, LORD, and come down; touch the mountains so that they smoke" — haṭ-šāmêkā wĕrēd (bow your heavens and come down) — the Sinai theophany vocabulary: YHWH descending in cloud, fire, and earthquake (Exod 19:18; Ps 18:9). gaʿ behārîm wĕyeʿăšānû (touch the mountains and they will smoke) — volcanic-theophany imagery; YHWH's mere touch causes mountains to smoke (Ps 104:32). The psalmist petitions for a repetition of the Exodus theophany, YHWH coming down into the present crisis.
6: bĕrôq bārāq ûtĕpîṣēm šelaḥ ḥiṣṣêkā ûtĕhummēm — "send out lightning and scatter them; shoot your arrows and put them to flight" — bĕrôq bārāq (flash lightning and scatter) — divine lightning as arrows (Ps 18:14; 77:17-18). šelaḥ ḥiṣṣêkā ûtĕhummēm (shoot your arrows and throw them into confusion) — hāmam (to confuse, rout, throw into panic) — the verb used for YHWH confusing the Egyptian army at the Red Sea (Exod 14:24). The petition is for YHWH to act as he did at the Exodus.
7: šĕlaḥ yādêkā mimmārôm pĕṣēnî wĕhaṣṣîlēnî mimmayim rabbîm miyyad bĕnê nēkār — "reach down your hand from above; rescue me and deliver me from the overwhelming waters, from the hand of foreigners" — šelaḥ yādêkā mimmārôm (send your hand from on high) — YHWH's hand reaching down from the heavens. mayim rabbîm (the great/overwhelming waters) — the primordial chaos waters (Ps 18:16; 93:4) as the image of the enemies' power. bĕnê nēkār (sons of foreignness, foreign people) — those outside the covenant, characteristically faithless to YHWH (v8's characterization follows).
8: ʾăšer pîhem dibbĕr-šāwĕʾ wîmînām yĕmîn šāqer — "whose mouths speak emptiness and whose right hand is a hand of deception" — šāwĕʾ (emptiness, vanity, nothingness, falsehood) — the same word as the third commandment (Exod 20:7: do not take the name of the LORD in vain/šāwĕʾ). Their speech is structurally empty, not merely accidentally false. yĕmîn šāqer (a right hand of deception) — the right hand used for oaths; a deceptive right hand is a perjured oath-hand. The foreigners are characterized by false speech and false oath — the opposite of YHWH whose word is ʾĕmet (truth) and whose oath is sure.
9: ʾĕlōhîm šîr ḥādāš ʾāšîr lĕkā bĕnēbel ʿāśôr ʾăzammĕrāh-llāk — "I will sing a new song to you, O God; on a ten-stringed instrument I will sing your praise" — šîr ḥādāš (new song) — the same formula as Ps 33:3; 96:1; 98:1; 149:1; Isa 42:10; Rev 5:9; 14:3. A new song for a new act of divine salvation; the expected rescue (vv5-7) calls forth praise before it fully arrives. nēbel ʿāśôr (a ten-stringed lute/harp) — a specific instrument (Ps 33:2; 92:3), possibly with ten strings arranged in a specific tuning.
10: hannôtēn tĕšûʿāh lammĕlākhîm hampoṣeh ʾet-dāwid ʿabdô mēḥereb rāʿāh — "who gives victory to kings and rescues his servant David from the deadly sword" — hannôtēn tĕšûʿāh lammĕlākhîm (the one who gives victory/salvation to kings) — YHWH as the source of royal military victory; the king's success is not his own but YHWH's gift. dāwid ʿabdô (his servant David) — naming David explicitly in the body of the psalm alongside "kings" in general places David within a broader theology of divine royal protection.
11: pĕṣēnî wĕhaṣṣîlēnî miyyad-bĕnê nēkār ʾăšer pîhem dibbĕr-šāwĕʾ wîmînām yĕmîn šāqer — nearly verbatim repetition of vv7b-8. The repetition creates a refrain-structure: rescue petition (v7) + enemy characterization (v8) → new song (v9-10) → rescue petition + enemy characterization (v11) → covenant blessing vision (vv12-15). The refrain about the foreigners' empty speech and false oath frames the covenant-blessing section as the content that is true, in contrast to the foreigners' falseness.
12: ʾăšer bānênû kĕnĕṭîʿîm mĕguddalîm binĕʿûrêhem bĕnôtênû kĕzāwiyyōt mĕḥuṭṭābôt tabbĕnît hêkāl — "may our sons be like vigorous plants in their youth; may our daughters be like carved corner pillars of a palace" — kĕnĕṭîʿîm mĕguddalîm (like plants nurtured/grown large) — the agricultural growth metaphor for human flourishing (Ps 128:3). kĕzāwiyyōt mĕḥuṭṭābôt tabbĕnît hêkāl (like corner pillars carved after the pattern of a palace/temple) — zāwiyyōt (corner pillars, angles) — the daughters are compared to architectural caryatids or atlantes, the carved human-figure columns that supported ancient temple porticoes. The comparison is one of strength, beauty, and load-bearing function — daughters who hold up the household.
13: mĕzāwênû mĕlēʾîm mĕpîqîm mizzan ʾel-zan ṣōʾnênû maalîpôt mĕrubbābôt bĕḥûṣôtênû — "may our storehouses be full with every kind of supply; may our flocks produce thousands and ten thousands in our open country" — the covenant blessing catalog: mĕzāwênû mĕlēʾîm (our storehouses/granaries full) — the grain that represents security. maalîpôt mĕrubbābôt (producing in the ten thousands, from ʾālap, thousand, and rābāh, to be many) — the exponential multiplication of flocks echoes the Deuteronomic blessings of Deut 28:4-5 ("the increase of your livestock").
14: ʾallûpênû mĕsubbālîm ʾên-pereṣ wĕʾên yôṣēʾt wĕʾên ṣĕwāḥāh birhōbōtênû — "may our cattle be well-laden/strong for labor; may there be no breach and no going out, no cry of distress in our streets" — mĕsubbālîm (laden, carrying loads, from sābal, to carry a heavy load) — work-cattle able to bear their loads. ʾên-pereṣ (no breach in the wall) — a broken-through city wall signals military defeat and exile. wĕʾên yôṣēʾt (no going out, no exile) — citizens not being taken captive. ʾên ṣĕwāḥāh birhōbōtênû (no cry of distress in our streets) — the broad street (rĕḥôb) is the public space where distress becomes visible and audible; its silence signals peace.
15: ʾašrê hāʿām šekkākāh llô ʾašrê hāʿām šeyhwāh ʾĕlōhāyw — "blessed are the people for whom it is so; blessed are the people whose God is the LORD" — the double beatitude closes the covenant-blessing section. šekkākāh llô (for whom it is so, lit. "that it is thus to him") — the material blessings of vv12-14. ʾašrê hāʿām šeyhwāh ʾĕlōhāyw (blessed are the people whose God is the LORD) — the second beatitude deepens the first: the real blessing is not the material catalog but the relationship with YHWH that generates it. A people whose God is the LORD have the source of blessing, not merely its expressions.
